editor_settings.title: Is the Fujifilm X100VI Hard to Get? Yes, and That Is Part of the Story
editor_settings.content:
Quick Verdict

The Fujifilm X100VI is hard to get, expensive, and still one of the most tempting compact cameras you can buy. Fujifilm gave it a 40.2MP APS-C sensor, X-Processor 5, 5-axis IBIS, a fixed 23mm f/2 lens, and 6.2K video, which is a big jump for a camera this small. The catch is simple: demand has stayed ahead of supply, and current retail listings still show it sitting at $1,799 and out of stock at major sellers like B&H.

Who This Camera Is For

This camera makes sense for people who want a premium everyday camera that feels fun, stylish, and easy to carry. It is especially good for travel, street photography, and creators who want Fujifilm color, film simulations, and a camera that looks good without trying too hard. The hybrid viewfinder, internal ND filter, and compact fixed-lens design make it feel more intentional than a regular mirrorless body with a zoom lens.

Who Should Skip It

Skip it if you mainly want value, flexibility, or a camera that you can actually find on the shelf without a hunt. If you need interchangeable lenses, the X100VI is the wrong shape of camera. If you do not care about the fixed 35mm-equivalent look, there are cheaper Fujifilm bodies that give you a lot of the same image quality without the hype tax.

Design and Build

The X100VI still wins people over the moment they pick it up. Fujifilm leans hard into the tactile experience with an aluminum top plate, physical dials, and a body that feels like a real object, not just another black rectangle. It is also compact enough to carry daily, but it is not toy-like. This is a camera that looks expensive because it is expensive.

Specs That Actually Matter

The specs that matter most here are the 40.2MP sensor, the 23mm f/2 lens, the 6.0-stop IBIS, the internal ND filter, and the 6.2K video support. Those are not random numbers for spec sheets, they change how the camera works in real life. The extra resolution gives you room to crop, the stabilization helps in low light, and the ND filter makes bright-day shooting easier without extra gear.


Real-World Performance

In real use, the X100VI is about speed, convenience, and confidence. It is quick enough for spontaneous shooting, sharp enough for serious work, and small enough that people actually carry it instead of leaving it at home. Fujifilm’s own positioning and feature breakdown make it clear that this camera is built for both stills and video, not just nostalgia. That said, the fixed lens means you must like the 35mm-equivalent look, because you are choosing the framing before you even start shooting.

The Hype Factor

A big part of the X100VI story is hype, and this one is real. Compact camera shipments rose strongly in 2025, and reporting linked that comeback to viral social media trends, including models like the X100VI. That is why this camera feels more like a cultural object than a normal product. It is not just being bought by photographers, it is being chased by everyone who wants that Fujifilm look and that pocketable luxury vibe.

FUJIFILM X100VI、写真も動画も綺麗に撮れて本当に良いカメラだ〜 pic.twitter.com/gby7elIcxo

— みちログ (@GadgetMitchy) April 3, 2026


That reaction matches the general mood around the X100VI. People do not talk about it like a boring tool. They talk about it like a camera that makes everyday shooting feel special, and that emotional pull is a huge part of why it stays in demand.

TikTok Clip

@taliapiperni My full review of the Fujifilm x100vi as a beginner #digitalcamera #photographyeveryday #beginnercamera #fujifilm #fujifilmx100vi #camerareview #amateurphotographer ♬ Jazz Bossa Nova - TOKYO Lonesome Blue


The TikTok angle makes sense too, because this camera is built for short-form appeal. It is small, handsome, and easy to show off, which is exactly the kind of product that spreads fast on social platforms. The X100VI is not just being reviewed, it is being performed.

Pricing Reality Check

Here is the honest part. Fujifilm raised the X100VI’s U.S. price from $1,599 to $1,799, and current retailer listings still show it at that higher price while remaining out of stock. TechRadar also reported that resellers pushed prices far above normal levels at times, with some listings climbing to around $5,000 on auction sites. In other words, the official price already hurts, and the hype market can hurt a lot more.

If you want a cheaper escape hatch, the used market has more sensible options. MPB lists used Fujifilm X100V bodies from €1,339 to €1,489, and B&H currently shows a used Fujifilm X-T30 II starting at $924.95. Those are not the same experience as an X100VI, but they are the kind of listings that make the VI look like a premium purchase rather than a smart bargain.

[product-card:fujifilm-x100vi-silver-camera-extras-1600-shutter-count-excellent-condition]


X100V vs X100VI

If you are choosing between the X100V and X100VI, the newer model is the better camera on paper and in practice. The X100VI brings a 40.2MP sensor, IBIS, and 6.2K video, while the X100V has a 26.1MP sensor, no IBIS, and 4K video. The X100V is lighter, and it still has the same basic fixed-lens charm, but the X100VI is the one that feels like the complete version.

Is It Worth It?

Yes, but only if you actually want what this camera is selling. The X100VI is worth it for people who care about the experience as much as the image quality, and who are happy paying for a compact camera that is part tool, part obsession. If you just want the smartest purchase, the answer gets less exciting fast. The price is high, the stock is thin, and the hype is real, but the camera itself is also genuinely very good.

Final Verdict

The Fujifilm X100VI is hard to get because it is one of those rare products where the hype matches the product quality more than people expect. It shoots beautifully, feels special, and nails the compact camera fantasy better than most rivals. Just do not buy into the idea that scarcity makes it automatically worth any price. It is good, but it is still a camera
